title: Paths of Power: Life in Edo Japan
description: Choose life as a samurai, shogun, or artisan during the Edo period and navigate honor, hierarchy, isolation, economic change, and political stability under the Tokugawa shogunate.

coverImage.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

teacherNotes: This deep-tree adventure uses three role-based openings that later merge into shared social, political, and economic crises. Students explore how Edo Japan preserved stability while facing tensions from samurai debt, merchant wealth, artisan labor, daimyo control, isolation, and urban growth.
reviewFocus: how social hierarchy, political control, isolation, and economic change shaped life during the Tokugawa shogunate
topicQuestion: how did feudalism and social hierarchy shape life in Edo Japan?

start.title: Three Roads in Edo Japan

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

start.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You live in Japan during the Edo period, when the Tokugawa shogunate rules from Edo.
The land is peaceful compared to the age of civil wars, but peace has a price. Everyone is expected to know their place.
A samurai is supposed to serve with honor. A shogun is supposed to preserve order. An artisan is supposed to create useful goods without challenging those above them.
Your story begins with a choice of role. Each path will reveal a different part of Japan's strict social hierarchy.

choices.text: Live as a young samurai serving a daimyo in a castle town.
choices.hint: This path explores bushido, duty, status, and adaptation during peace.

choices.text: Rule as a shogun trying to control daimyo and preserve stability.
choices.hint: This path explores political control, isolation, and state power.

choices.text: Work as an artisan in a growing city where skill brings respect but not high status.
choices.hint: This path explores urban life, class limits, trade, and economic change.

samurai-1.title: The Sword in a Time of Peace

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-1.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You are Ren, the child of a samurai family. Your father served your daimyo when swords still decided power.
Now the castle town is peaceful. The streets are full of merchants, paper lanterns, apprentices, and officials carrying records instead of weapons.
You wear the swords of your class, but you wonder what a warrior is supposed to become when there is no war.

choices.text: Train harder and prove your loyalty through strict bushido.
choices.hint: Honor may protect your status.

choices.text: Study reading, writing, and accounting to serve as an official.
choices.hint: Peace may require a new kind of service.

choices.text: Spend time in the markets and learn how merchants are gaining wealth.
choices.hint: Economic change may challenge old ideas about status.

samurai-duty-2.title: The Practice Yard

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-duty-2.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: Each morning, you train with wooden swords until your palms sting.
Your instructor repeats that bushido means loyalty, discipline, and self-control.
Yet when you walk home, you pass shops where merchants count coins while samurai families quietly sell heirlooms to survive.

choices.text: Stay focused on honor and ignore the market's temptations.
choices.hint: This preserves tradition but may ignore economic pressure.

choices.text: Ask your instructor why samurai are poor while merchants grow wealthy.
choices.hint: This may open a difficult discussion.

choices.text: Secretly borrow money from a merchant to protect your family's status.
choices.hint: Debt may solve one problem while creating another.

samurai-scholar-2.title: Brush Instead of Blade

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-scholar-2.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You study law codes, rice records, and Neo-Confucian teachings about order and duty.
Some older warriors say a samurai who spends too much time with books grows soft.
But your daimyo needs officials who can manage taxes, settle disputes, and report to the shogunate.

choices.text: Become a clerk and help manage rice taxes fairly.
choices.hint: This adapts samurai service to government work.

choices.text: Use your learning to question unfair class rules.
choices.hint: This may challenge the social hierarchy.

choices.text: Hide your interest in scholarship and return to weapons training.
choices.hint: This returns to the traditional samurai path.

samurai-market-2.title: Coins in the Market

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-market-2.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: The market is louder than the training yard. Artisans sell lacquerware, paper fans, and woodblock prints.
Merchants bow politely to samurai, but everyone knows many merchants have more money than the warriors who outrank them.
A merchant offers to teach you how credit works. Your family could use the help.

choices.text: Accept the lesson but keep your distance.
choices.hint: Learning about money may help, but it risks your honor.

choices.text: Visit an artisan workshop to understand city life better.
choices.hint: This connects your samurai path to the artisan world.

choices.text: Report the merchant for disrespecting the social order.
choices.hint: Defending hierarchy may preserve status but create resentment.

samurai-patrol-3.title: Patrol Through the Castle Town

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-patrol-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You patrol the castle town wearing your swords with pride.
A crowd gathers outside a rice broker's storehouse. Peasants complain that rice payments are unfair, while merchants say prices are rising.
You have authority, but using it carelessly could turn hunger into unrest.

choices.text: Calm the crowd and investigate the rice records.
choices.hint: Evidence may prevent unrest.

choices.text: Threaten punishment until everyone leaves.
choices.hint: Force may restore order briefly.

choices.text: Protect the merchant because he has lent money to samurai families.
choices.hint: Personal interest may damage trust.

samurai-question-3.title: Questions No One Wants

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-question-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You ask why status does not match wealth, and why duty does not always feed a family.
Your teacher warns you that Edo society depends on each class accepting its role.
Still, even he admits that peace has changed the meaning of samurai life.

choices.text: Argue for reform while staying loyal to your daimyo.
choices.hint: Careful reform may be possible.

choices.text: Keep your questions private and serve quietly.
choices.hint: Quiet service may still help people.

choices.text: Reject the system and consider becoming a ronin.
choices.hint: Leaving the system is dangerous.

samurai-debt-3.title: The Merchant's Ledger

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-debt-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: The merchant writes your family's name into a ledger.
The loan saves your household from embarrassment, but it also gives a lower-status merchant quiet power over you.
In Edo Japan, status and wealth do not always point in the same direction.

choices.text: Repay the debt honestly by taking official work.
choices.hint: Duty and discipline may restore balance.

choices.text: Use your status to intimidate the merchant into silence.
choices.hint: Abusing status may lead to a bad path.

choices.text: Ask the merchant to introduce you to artisan producers.
choices.hint: This may reveal how the city economy works.

samurai-order-3.title: Defending the Old Order

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-order-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You report the merchant's boldness to a local official.
The merchant bows and apologizes, but his eyes show anger.
The social hierarchy remains intact on the surface, yet pressure builds underneath.

choices.text: Use the moment to call for stricter class rules.
choices.hint: This may support order but increase resentment.

choices.text: Realize punishment alone will not solve economic change.
choices.hint: A more careful response may be needed.

choices.text: Continue using your rank to silence criticism.
choices.hint: Power without trust can become dangerous.

samurai-rice-records-3.title: Rice Records

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-rice-records-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You copy rice tax records by lamplight. Names, fields, harvests, payments.
The rice economy supports samurai stipends, daimyo obligations, and the shogunate's control.
One set of numbers looks wrong. Someone is hiding grain or shifting the burden onto weaker families.

choices.text: Investigate the records carefully.
choices.hint: Evidence can reveal the problem.

choices.text: Ignore the error to avoid angering powerful people.
choices.hint: Silence may protect you but harm others.

choices.text: Warn your daimyo that unfair taxes could create unrest.
choices.hint: Leaders may respond if they understand the danger.

samurai-crackdown-4.title: Order by Fear

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-crackdown-4.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You raise your voice and order the crowd to scatter.
For a moment, the street empties. But the anger does not disappear. It moves indoors, behind screens and closed doors.
Fear can quiet a town. It cannot make people trust you.

choices.text: Report that order has been restored and move on.
choices.hint: Ignoring causes may lead to instability.

choices.text: Return later and listen to the complaints.
choices.hint: It may still be possible to repair trust.

choices.text: Push for harsher punishments against dissent.
choices.hint: Stricter control may preserve order or deepen resentment.

samurai-corruption-4.title: A Favor Repaid

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-corruption-4.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You protect the merchant who helped your family.
He smiles with relief, but others notice. Soon people whisper that samurai justice can be bought.
Your honor is not lost in one moment. It begins to fray thread by thread.

choices.text: Confess the conflict of interest to your daimyo.
choices.hint: Honesty may restore honor.

choices.text: Continue accepting favors from the merchant.
choices.hint: Corruption can damage social order.

choices.text: Break ties and investigate the dispute fairly.
choices.hint: You may still repair the damage.

samurai-silence-4.title: The Safe Silence

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-silence-4.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You close the ledger and say nothing.
No one criticizes you. No one punishes you. Your family remains safe for now.
But the families paying unfairly will remember who had the power to speak and chose silence.

choices.text: Stay silent and protect your household.
choices.hint: This leads to a limited but safe outcome.

choices.text: Change your mind and warn the daimyo.
choices.hint: It may still be possible to act.

choices.text: Quietly give the information to an artisan family affected by the taxes.
choices.hint: This connects your path to lower-status townspeople.

samurai-reform-4.title: Honor Reimagined

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-reform-4.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You argue that bushido should mean more than obedience.
If a samurai protects order while ignoring suffering, is that honor or pride?
Some younger samurai listen. Older officials warn that too much change invites disorder.

choices.text: Work within the system to correct unfair rice records.
choices.hint: Reform through evidence may succeed.

choices.text: Teach young samurai that service now requires learning and administration.
choices.hint: This can lead to a good samurai ending.

choices.text: Speak too openly against your superiors.
choices.hint: Reform without caution may cost your position.

samurai-ronin-4.title: The Road of the Ronin

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

samurai-ronin-4.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You imagine leaving your lord's service and becoming a ronin, a masterless samurai.
Freedom calls to you, but so does hunger. Without a lord, your swords may bring fear instead of respect.
A society built on hierarchy has little room for someone who steps outside it.

choices.text: Seek a teaching position and live humbly.
choices.hint: This may preserve dignity outside warfare.

choices.text: Sell your sword to whoever pays.
choices.hint: This path risks violence and dishonor.

choices.text: Return to your daimyo and accept disciplined service.
choices.hint: You may still serve with a new understanding.

daimyo-warning-5.title: Warning the Daimyo

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

daimyo-warning-5.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You kneel before your daimyo and explain the danger.
Unfair rice records, merchant debt, and hungry townspeople are not separate problems. Together, they threaten stability.
Your daimyo listens, but he also fears looking weak before the shogunate.

choices.text: Recommend fair tax review and calm investigation.
choices.hint: Careful reform may prevent unrest.

choices.text: Recommend stricter surveillance of townspeople.
choices.hint: Control may preserve order but raise fear.

choices.text: Recommend hiding the problem from Edo.
choices.hint: Covering up weakness may make collapse worse.

shogun-1.title: The Weight of Edo

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

shogun-1.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You are Ietsuna, heir to the authority of the Tokugawa shogunate.
From Edo, your officials watch daimyo domains, castle towns, roads, ports, temples, and markets.
Peace has lasted because power is carefully balanced. A careless decision could awaken old rivalries.

choices.text: Focus on controlling the daimyo.
choices.hint: Daimyo power is the greatest political threat.

choices.text: Focus on isolation policies and foreign influence.
choices.hint: Sakoku helps limit outside disruption.

choices.text: Focus on economic problems in castle towns.
choices.hint: Urban growth and merchant wealth challenge hierarchy.

shogun-daimyo-2.title: Daimyo Under Watch

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

shogun-daimyo-2.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: Daimyo rule their domains, but they must respect the shogun's authority.
Your advisors recommend strict attendance in Edo, costly travel, and careful monitoring of marriages and castle repairs.
Control keeps peace, but too much pressure may create quiet resentment.

choices.text: Strengthen alternate attendance requirements.
choices.hint: This keeps daimyo close and costly.

choices.text: Trust loyal daimyo with more freedom.
choices.hint: Trust may reduce resentment but weaken control.

choices.text: Use spies to watch suspicious domains.
choices.hint: Information can protect power, but fear spreads.

shogun-isolation-2.title: The Closed Gate

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

shogun-isolation-2.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: Reports arrive of foreign traders and missionaries seeking influence near Japan's coasts.
Your advisors warn that outside ideas could weaken loyalty and disturb the social order.
Others argue that controlled trade could bring knowledge, silver, medicine, and weapons.

choices.text: Maintain strict sakoku policies.
choices.hint: Isolation may protect stability.

choices.text: Allow limited trade through controlled ports.
choices.hint: Careful contact may bring benefits.

choices.text: Open trade more widely to increase wealth.
choices.hint: Rapid change may destabilize hierarchy.

shogun-economy-2.title: The Problem of Peace

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

shogun-economy-2.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: Peace has filled castle towns with artisans, merchants, theaters, restaurants, and schools.
Samurai still rank above merchants, but merchants increasingly control loans, goods, and urban wealth.
The social hierarchy says one thing. The economy says another.

choices.text: Regulate merchants and protect samurai status.
choices.hint: This defends hierarchy.

choices.text: Encourage urban growth while watching for unrest.
choices.hint: Economic activity may strengthen society.

choices.text: Ignore the economy because political order matters more.
choices.hint: Unaddressed pressure may grow.

alternate-attendance-3.title: Roads to Edo

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

alternate-attendance-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: Daimyo processions travel to Edo with retainers, supplies, and symbols of loyalty.
The cost keeps daimyo from building private armies, but it also drains their domains.
Peasants along the roads provide labor and goods. Stability rests on many tired backs.

choices.text: Keep the policy but reduce unfair burdens on villages.
choices.hint: Balanced rule may preserve legitimacy.

choices.text: Increase the costs to weaken daimyo further.
choices.hint: This may prevent rebellion but create suffering.

choices.text: Use the roads to improve communication and trade.
choices.hint: Infrastructure can support a larger system.

daimyo-freedom-3.title: Trusting the Lords

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

daimyo-freedom-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You loosen restrictions on loyal daimyo.
They praise your wisdom in public, but your advisors worry that freedom can become ambition.
In a feudal system, loyalty often depends on what power can enforce.

choices.text: Grant freedom only to domains with proven loyalty.
choices.hint: Selective trust may work.

choices.text: Let daimyo manage themselves without interference.
choices.hint: This risks weakening the shogunate.

choices.text: Pair freedom with closer economic reporting.
choices.hint: Records may replace some direct control.

spy-network-3.title: Eyes in Every Province

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

spy-network-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: Messengers, inspectors, and informants send reports to Edo.
You learn which daimyo spend too much, which samurai complain, and which towns grow restless.
Information gives power, but people who feel watched may stop offering honest advice.

choices.text: Use reports to solve problems before unrest begins.
choices.hint: Information can support good government.

choices.text: Punish suspicious domains harshly.
choices.hint: Fear may enforce obedience.

choices.text: Spy on merchants and artisans as well.
choices.hint: Broad control may increase resentment.

sakoku-edict-3.title: The Sakoku Edict

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

sakoku-edict-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You strengthen the policy of sakoku, limiting foreign contact and controlling trade.
Officials praise the decision. Religious and political threats from outside are reduced.
Yet some scholars whisper that Japan may fall behind if it learns too little from the wider world.

choices.text: Allow carefully controlled Dutch and Chinese trade at one port.
choices.hint: Limited exchange may preserve control.

choices.text: Ban nearly all foreign books and contact.
choices.hint: This protects order but limits knowledge.

choices.text: Use isolation to strengthen internal roads, schools, and cities.
choices.hint: Internal development may balance limited outside contact.

controlled-port-3.title: A Narrow Window

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

controlled-port-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You allow limited trade through a tightly controlled port.
Foreign goods, maps, medicines, and ideas enter slowly, watched by officials.
A narrow window can bring light. It can also let in a storm if left unguarded.

choices.text: Study useful foreign knowledge while limiting political influence.
choices.hint: Careful learning may benefit Japan.

choices.text: Let merchants expand the trade quietly.
choices.hint: Merchant wealth may challenge status.

choices.text: Close the window again after rumors spread.
choices.hint: Fear may limit opportunity.

wide-trade-3.title: Too Many Ships

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

wide-trade-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: Ports become crowded with foreign goods and unfamiliar ideas.
Merchants grow rich quickly. Some daimyo seek private deals.
The flow of trade is exciting, but it moves faster than your officials can control.

choices.text: Pull back and create controlled trade rules.
choices.hint: A correction may restore balance.

choices.text: Allow daimyo to trade independently.
choices.hint: Private wealth may weaken central control.

choices.text: Tax the trade heavily to fund the shogunate.
choices.hint: This may bring revenue but anger merchants.

merchant-regulation-3.title: Rules for the Market

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

merchant-regulation-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You issue regulations on merchant clothing, prices, and public display of wealth.
Samurai families approve. Merchants bow and obey in public.
But trade does not disappear because a law dislikes it.

choices.text: Regulate luxury while allowing useful trade.
choices.hint: Balance may preserve order.

choices.text: Punish merchants who appear too wealthy.
choices.hint: This may increase resentment.

choices.text: Create official channels for loans and rice exchange.
choices.hint: Managing the economy may reduce conflict.

urban-growth-3.title: The Expanding City

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

urban-growth-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: Edo grows larger and louder.
Artisans make goods for samurai households. Merchants move rice, cloth, paper, and tools. Entertainment districts draw crowds.
Urbanization creates opportunity, but also anxiety among officials.

choices.text: Support urban growth with rules for fire safety, roads, and markets.
choices.hint: Public order can support city life.

choices.text: Encourage artisan production for domestic trade.
choices.hint: This connects political policy to artisan life.

choices.text: Fear urban culture and restrict public gatherings.
choices.hint: Restriction may protect order but limit growth.

ignored-economy-3.title: The Problem Left Alone

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

ignored-economy-3.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: You decide that class order matters more than economic complaints.
Samurai debt grows. Merchant wealth grows. Artisans work longer hours for uncertain pay.

final-reform-council.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: Representatives gather in a formal hall. Samurai sit closest to authority. Merchants and artisans wait farther back.
The question is not whether Edo society will remain hierarchical. It will.
The question is whether leaders can preserve order while responding to the changes peace has created.

choices.text: Balance hierarchy with fair records, controlled trade, and protections for townspeople.
choices.hint: This leads to a good ending.

choices.text: Preserve hierarchy but allow limited economic protections.
choices.hint: This leads to a neutral ending.

choices.text: Promise reform but change almost nothing.
choices.hint: False reform may deepen mistrust.

final-compromise-council.title: The Compromise

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

final-compromise-council.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: The final decision avoids dramatic change.
Samurai status remains protected. Merchants face rules but keep working. Artisans gain small protections, not real power.
The compromise is not justice. It is survival within limits.

choices.text: Accept the compromise as the price of peace.
choices.hint: This leads to a neutral ending.

choices.text: Use the compromise as a foundation for gradual improvement.
choices.hint: This can lead to a good ending.

choices.text: Let powerful groups quietly ignore the compromise.
choices.hint: Weak enforcement may cause unrest.

final-strain-council.title: Pressure Under the Floorboards

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

final-strain-council.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: The council ends with polite words, but little trust.
Samurai debt remains. Merchant resentment remains. Artisan frustration remains. Daimyo domains remain strained.
The floorboards of Edo society do not break today, but they creak beneath everyone's feet.

choices.text: Reopen the review before anger spreads.
choices.hint: It may still be possible to act.

choices.text: Use surveillance and punishment to force quiet.
choices.hint: Fear may create a bad ending.

choices.text: Accept a tense but functioning society.
choices.hint: This leads to a neutral ending.

good-stable-reform.title: Peace With Listening

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

good-stable-reform.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: The shogunate keeps order, but it also listens more carefully.
Rice records are reviewed. Merchant credit is regulated. Artisan contracts become clearer. Samurai service expands into administration and education.
No one mistakes Edo Japan for an equal society, but the system becomes more stable because leaders respond to real problems.
good-stable-reform.teachingSummary: This ending shows that the Tokugawa shogunate preserved stability through hierarchy, control, and administration. It also shows that long-lasting order required leaders to manage economic change, class tension, and urban growth.
good-stable-reform.keyTermReview: Vocabulary review: This adventure connects the Tokugawa shogunate and Edo period to Japan's strict social hierarchy. Students examine how the shogun controlled daimyo, how samurai followed bushido while adapting to peace, how artisans and merchants shaped castle towns and urbanization, and how policies such as isolation and sakoku helped preserve order while limiting outside influence.

The society remains hierarchical, but it survives because it adapts without tearing itself apart.
good-gradual-change.teachingSummary: This ending shows how Edo Japan balanced continuity and change. The social hierarchy remained strict, but peaceful adaptation helped castle towns, artisans, merchants, and samurai function within the Tokugawa system.

Foreign influence remains limited, but internal administration improves.
The Tokugawa peace holds because authority is organized, watchful, and practical.

Doctors, translators, and officials study carefully without allowing foreign powers to dominate politics.
The gate stays narrow, but it does not fully close.

You teach younger samurai that bushido can include honesty, learning, and protection of the people.
Peace has changed the warrior class, and you change with it.

Some unfair records are corrected. Some contracts improve. But samurai still stand above artisans and merchants, even when merchants hold more wealth.
Life continues, stable but unequal.
neutral-order-limits.teachingSummary: This ending shows that Edo Japan's social hierarchy created stability while limiting social mobility. Many people lived secure but restricted lives within assigned class roles.

The town returns to work, but complaints remain beneath the surface.
The Tokugawa order survives today. Tomorrow will bring the same questions back again.

Peace survives, but so do limits.

bad-daimyo-revolt.teachingSummary: This ending shows why the Tokugawa shogunate carefully controlled daimyo. Central authority and daimyo restrictions were key to maintaining peace.
bad-daimyo-revolt.keyTermReview: Vocabulary review: This adventure connects the Tokugawa shogunate and Edo period to Japan's strict social hierarchy. Students examine how the shogun controlled daimyo, how samurai followed bushido while adapting to peace, how artisans and merchants shaped castle towns and urbanization, and how policies such as isolation and sakoku helped preserve order while limiting outside influence.
reflectionQuestions: How did your role in Edo society shape the choices available to you?

bad-punishment.title: Punished for Speaking

image.alt: A Japanese castle town with a samurai, shogun officials, and artisan workshops during the Edo period

bad-punishment.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: Tokugawa shogunate, Edo period, shogun, samurai, daimyo, bushido, artisan, merchant, social hierarchy, isolation, sakoku, rice economy, castle town, urbanization, and Neo-Confucianism.
body: Officials decide that your challenge threatens public order.
The workshop is fined. Leaders are punished. Others learn to stay silent.
Your complaint was not meaningless, but the system was not ready to hear it.
bad-punishment.teachingSummary: This ending shows how difficult it was for lower-status groups to challenge the social hierarchy. Edo society valued order and often punished open disruption.
